Name: Apache ActiveMQ Files Exposed
description: The web application exposes files or directories that are accessible to unauthorized users. This can lead to information disclosure, source code leakage, or even remote code execution in some cases.
detection_result: The scanner found exposed files or directories on the web server.
detection_method: The scanner performed an HTTP request to the target URL and analyzed the response. If the response indicates that a file or directory is accessible (e.g., 200 OK, directory listing), it is flagged as exposed.
impact: An attacker can access sensitive files, configuration files, source code, or directories that may contain additional vulnerabilities or information useful for further attacks.
solution: Restrict access to sensitive files and directories using proper authentication and authorization mechanisms. Implement access control lists (ACLs) and ensure that the web server configuration does not allow directory listing.
insight: Exposed files and directories are a common misconfiguration that can be easily exploited if not properly secured.
log_method: The scanner logs the HTTP request and response, including the status code and any exposed content.
references: CWE-552: Files or Directories Accessible to External Parties

product_detection_result: Method: Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP)
impact: Aected Software/OS
Eclipse Jetty version 9.0.0 through 9.4.53, 10.0.0 through 10.0.17, 11.0.0 through 11.0.17 and
insight: There exists a security vulnerability in Jetty's DosFilter which can be exploited by unauthorized
users to cause remote denial of service (DoS) attack on the server using DosFilter. By repeatedly
sending crafted requests, attackers can trigger OutofMemory errors and exhaust the server's
memory nally.

Name: Eclipse Jetty Privilege Escalation Vulnerability - Linux
description: Eclipse Jetty is prone to a privilege escalation vulnerability through a local temp directory.
detection_method: Checks if a vulnerable version is present on the target host.
product_detection_result: Detected by Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP) (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.800953)
impact: Aected Software/OS Eclipse Jetty version 9.4.32.v20200930 and prior, 10.0.0.beta2 and prior and 11.0.0.beta2 and prior.
insight: On Unix like systems, the system's temporary directory is shared between all users on that system. A collocated user can observe the process of creating a temporary sub directory in the shared temporary directory and race to complete the creation of the temporary subdirectory.

If the attacker wins the race then they will have read and write permission to the subdirectory used to unpack web applications, including their WEB-INF/lib jar les and JSP les. If any code is ever executed out of this temporary directory, this can lead to a local privilege escalation vulnerability.

Name: Eclipse Jetty Information Disclosure Vulnerability (GHSA-gwcr-j4wh-j3cq)
description: Eclipse Jetty is prone to an information disclosure vulnerability in the ConcatServlet and Wel- comeFilter servlet.
product_detection_result: Method: Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP)
impact: Eclipse Jetty prior to version 9.4.41, 10.x through 10.0.2 and 11.x through 11.0.2.
insight: Requests to the ConcatServlet and WelcomeFilter are able to access protected resources within the WEB-INF directory. For example a request to the ConcatServlet with a URI of /concat?/%2557EB-INF/web.xml can retrieve the web.xml le. This can reveal sensitive infor- mation regarding the implementation of a web application.

Name: Eclipse Jetty HTTP Header Vulnerability (GHSA-hmr7-m48g-48f6) - Linux
description: Eclipse Jetty is prone to an HTTP header vulnerability.
Quality of Detection (QoD): 30%
detection_method: Checks if a vulnerable version is present on the target host.
Details: Eclipse Jetty HTTP Header Vulnerability (GHSA-hmr7-m48g-48f6) - Linux
Version used: 2024-06-11T05:05:40Z
solution: Eclipse Jetty version 9.0.0 through 9.4.51, 10.0.0 through 10.0.15, 11.0.0 through 11.0.15 and
insight: Jetty accepts the '+' character proceeding the content-length value in a HTTP/1 header eld.
This is more permissive than allowed by the RFC and other servers routinely reject such requests
with 400 responses. There is no known exploit scenario, but it is conceivable that request
smuggling could result if jetty is used in combination with a server that does not close the
connection after sending such a 400 response.

Name: Apache ActiveMQ Fileserver Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
description: Apache ActiveMQ is an open source message broker written in Java together with a full Java Message Service (JMS) client. It provides 'Enterprise Features' which in this case includes exposing a fileserver to clients. The fileserver is used for fileserver update functionality. Multiple vulnerabilities exist that allow an attacker to upload arbitrary files to the fileserver or move them to other locations which may lead to code execution.
detection_result: The remote host is affected by the vulnerability.
detection_method: The detection is based on a banner check.
product_detection_result: The remote host is running Apache ActiveMQ version 5.14.0.
impact: An attacker may be able to upload arbitrary files to the fileserver or move them to other locations which may lead to code execution.
solution: Update to Apache ActiveMQ version 5.14.1 or later.
insight: The vulnerability is caused by insufficient input validation in the fileserver functionality.
log_method: The detection is based on a banner check.

Name: TCP Timestamps Information Disclosure
description: The remote host implements TCP timestamps and therefore allows to compute the uptime.
Quality of Detection (QoD): 80%
detection_result: It was detected that the host implements RFC1323/RFC7323.
The following timestamps were retrieved with a delay of 1 seconds in-between:
detection_method: Special IP packets are forged and sent with a little delay in between to the target IP. The responses are searched for a timestamps. If found, the timestamps are reported.
Details: TCP Timestamps Information Disclosure
Version used: 2023-12-15T16:10:08Z
impact: A side eect of this feature is that the uptime of the remote host can sometimes be computed.
solution: To disable TCP timestamps on linux add the line 'net.ipv4.tcp_timestamps = 0' to /etc/sysctl.conf. Execute 'sysctl -p' to apply the settings at runtime.
To disable TCP timestamps on Windows execute 'netsh int tcp set global timestamps=disabled'
Starting with Windows Server 2008 and Vista, the timestamp can not be completely disabled.

The default behavior of the TCP/IP stack on this Systems is to not use the Timestamp options when initiating TCP connections, but use them if the TCP peer that is initiating communication includes them in their synchronize (SYN) segment.
See the references for more information.
insight: The remote host implements TCP timestamps, as dened by RFC1323/RFC7323.

Name: Eclipse Jetty Session Vulnerability (GHSA-m6cp-vxjx-65j6) - Linux
description: Eclipse Jetty is prone to a vulnerability in the session management.
Quality of Detection (QoD): 30%
detection_method: Checks if a vulnerable version is present on the target host.
Details: Eclipse Jetty Session Vulnerability (GHSA-m6cp-vxjx-65j6) - Linux
Version used: 2024-06-11T05:05:40Z
product_detection_result: Detected by Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP) (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.800953)
Method: Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP)   OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.800953)
impact: If an exception is thrown from the SessionListener#sessionDestroyed() method, then the session ID is not invalidated in the session ID manager. On deployments with clustered sessions and multiple contexts this can result in a session not being invalidated. This can result in an application used on a shared computer being left logged in.
insight: If an exception is thrown from the SessionListener#sessionDestroyed() method, then the ses- sion ID is not invalidated in the session ID manager. On deployments with clustered sessions and multiple contexts this can result in a session not being invalidated. This can result in an application used on a shared computer being left logged in.

Name: Eclipse Jetty XXE Vulnerability (GHSA-58qw-p7qm-5rvh) - Linux
description: Eclipse Jetty is prone to a XML external entity (XXE) vulnerability in the XMLParser.
Quality of Detection (QoD): 30%
detection_method: Checks if a vulnerable version is present on the target host.
Details: Eclipse Jetty XXE Vulnerability (GHSA-58qw-p7qm-5rvh) - Linux
Version used: 2024-06-11T05:05:40Z
product_detection_result: Detected by Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP) (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.800953)
Method: Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP)
impact: XmlParser is being used when parsing Jetty's xml conguration les. An attacker might exploit this vulnerability in order to achieve SSRF or cause a denial of service.
One possible scenario is importing a (remote) malicious WAR into a Jetty's server, while the WAR includes a malicious web.xml.
insight: XmlParser is being used when parsing Jetty's xml conguration les. An attacker might exploit this vulnerability in order to achieve SSRF or cause a denial of service.
One possible scenario is importing a (remote) malicious WAR into a Jetty's server, while the WAR includes a malicious web.xml.

Name: Eclipse Jetty CgiServlet Vulnerability (GHSA-3gh6-v5v9-6v9j) - Linux
description: Eclipse Jetty is prone to a vulnerability in the CgiServlet.
Quality of Detection (QoD): 30%
detection_method: Checks if a vulnerable version is present on the target host.
Details: Eclipse Jetty CgiServlet Vulnerability (GHSA-3gh6-v5v9-6v9j) - Linux OID:1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.151003 Version used: 2025-05-28T05:40:15Z
product_detection_result: Detected by Mort Bay / Eclipse Jetty Detection (HTTP) (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.800953)
insight: Users of the CgiServlet with a very specific command structure may have the wrong command executed. If a user sends a request to a org.eclipse.jetty.servlets.CGI Servlet for a binary with a space in its name, the servlet will escape the command by wrapping it in quotation marks. This wrapped command, plus an optional command prefix, will then be executed through a call to Runtime.exec. If the original binary name provided by the user contains a quotation mark followed by a space, the resulting command line will contain multiple tokens instead of one.

Name: ICMP Timestamp Reply Information Disclosure
description: The remote host responded to an ICMP timestamp request.
Quality of Detection (QoD): 80%
detection_result: The following response / ICMP packet has been received:
detection_method: Sends an ICMP Timestamp (Type 13) request and checks if a Timestamp Reply (Type 14) is received.
Details: ICMP Timestamp Reply Information Disclosure
Version used: 2025-01-21T05:37:33Z
impact: This information could theoretically be used to exploit weak time-based random number generators in other services.
solution: - Disable the support for ICMP timestamp on the remote host completely
- Protect the remote host by a firewall, and block ICMP packets passing through the firewall in either direction (either completely or only for untrusted networks)
insight: The Timestamp Reply is an ICMP message which replies to a Timestamp message. It consists of the originating timestamp sent by the sender of the Timestamp as well as a receive timestamp and a transmit timestamp.

Name: Web Application Scanning Consolidation / Info Reporting
description: The script consolidates and reports various information for web application (formerly called 'CGI') scanning.
This information is based on the following scripts / settings:
- HTTP-Version Detection (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.100034)
- Web mirroring / webmirror.nasl (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.10662)
- Directory Scanner / DDI_Directory_Scanner.nasl (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.11032)
- The congured 'cgi_path' within the 'Scanner Preferences' of the scan cong in use

Name: Web Application Directory Scanner
description: The scanner has found a number of directories on the web server. While this is not, in and of itself, a bug, you should manually inspect these directories to ensure that they are in compliance with company security standards.
detection_result: The following directories were used for web application scanning: http://activemq514.trabalho_vulnnet:8161/, http://activemq514.trabalho_vulnnet:8161/demo
impact: An attacker may find sensitive information or functionality in these directories.
solution: Manually inspect the discovered directories and remove any that are not required for the application to function.

description: Enumerates which HTTP methods are allowed.
detection_result: The following list contains the URLs and corresponding supported HTTP methods.
insight: - Basic HTTP methods: GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, DELETE, CONNECT, OPTIONS, TRACE

Name: CPE Inventory
description: This routine uses information collected by other routines about CPE identities of operating systems, services and applications detected during the scan.

Note: Some CPEs for specic products might show up twice or more in the output. Background: After a product got renamed or a specic vendor was acquired by another one it might happen that a product gets a new CPE within the NVD CPE Dictionary but older entries are kept with the older CPE.

Name: Service Detection with 'GET' Request
description: This plugin performs service detection.
Quality of Detection (QoD): 80%
detection_result: An AMQP service seems to be running on this port.
insight: This plugin is a complement of the plugin 'Services' (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.25623.1.0.10330). It sends a HTTP 'GET' request to the remaining unknown services and tries to identify them.
